prevalence.source: Orphanet; European metabolic registries and newborn screening summaries
prevalence.trend: stable; earlier diagnosis improving with newborn screening and standardized emergency protocols

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare) but high per-patient morbidity (metabolic crises, cardiomyopathy, neurodisability)
burden.mortalityRate: Low with early diagnosis and emergency management; higher in severe neonatal cases

vulnerablePopulations: patients with recurrent infections triggering crises
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Newborn screening/MS/MS (elevated C3) with confirmatory urine organic acids (methylcitrate, 3-hydroxypropionate), plasma acylcarnitines, genetic confirmation

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: moderate to high depending on country; confirmatory and emergency pathways established in metabolic centers
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good when diagnosed early and managed in specialized metabolic clinics

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Newborn screening in many countries and rapid referral to metabolic centers
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emergency protocols, protein restriction/medical nutrition, carnitine, intermittent antibiotics for gut propionate, cardiology/neuro follow-up; transplant evaluation in select cases
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Cross-country variation in screening and access to specialized services
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Established metabolic networks and standardized emergency and dietary management in many systems
